P a g e  7 / 9  
 
3.3. Depth Preprocessing & Standardization Pipeline 
We have implemented the following preprocessing procedures: 
• Missing Value Handling (Inpainting): Raw depth maps from sensors often 
contain "black holes" (missing values) due to reflective surfaces or occlusion. 
We apply bilateral filtering and interpolation to fill these gaps before passing 
data to the navigation logic. 
• Unit Standardization: All depth outputs are converted to a unified metric

scale (meters). For models that provide relative depth (inverse depth), we 
apply a linear transformation based on known camera focal length to 
approximate metric distance. 
• RGB-Depth Alignment: Since the YOLOv8 detections (RGB) and Depth 
Anything predictions (Depth) must overlap perfectly, we use a joint-resizing 
strategy. Both inputs are transformed to 640 × 640 using the same 
transformation matrix to ensure that a bounding box at [𝑥, 𝑦] on the RGB

frame corresponds exactly to the depth value at [𝑥, 𝑦]. 
• Normalization: Depth values are normalized to a range of [0,1] during the 
internal risk calculation to ensure the EMA (Exponential Moving Average) in 
our logic remains stable regardless of sensor noise. 
 
3.4. Summary of Dataset Preparedness 
By selecting NYU-Depth V2 and the Depth Anything V2 architecture, the team has 
reduced the computational overhead while maintaining high precision for close-

range indoor obstacles. This standardized pipeline directly feeds into the 
NavigationDeterministicLogic verified in Milestone 3. 
 
4. Speech Synthesis Identification & Interaction Design 
4.1. Role of Speech Synthesis in Action-Oriented Navigation 
Unlike standard navigation apps that provide occasional turn-by-turn directions, this 
system requires High-Frequency, Low-Latency feedback. The speech module is 
the primary interface between the spatial logic and the user.

• Necessity: High-quality speech datasets (like LJSpeech and LibriTTS) are 
required to ensure that the synthesized voice remains intelligible even in 
noisy outdoor environments or when the user is moving quickly. 
• Functional Goal: To provide instructions in under 200ms from the moment 
an obstacle is detected, ensuring the user has time to react.
